post: What does courage look like when everything has gone wrong? At the Chosin Reservoir in November 1950, Task Force Faith faced -30 degree temperatures, Chinese forces outnumbering them 9 to 1, frozen weapons, exhausted medical supplies, and near-total communications failure. Their commander was captured. Their unit was largely destroyed. They kept fighting. In this episode of BRI Scholar Talks, historian Steve Vogel joins Tony Williams to examine what honor, courage, and sacrifice look like under the most extreme pressure, and why the men of Task Force Faith deserve to be remembered for the virtues they demonstrated, not the false accusations that followed. Watch it here:  https://bit.ly/4dALV8q  #KoreanWar  #BRIScholarTalks  #History

Standing armies without the consent of the legislature. For the Founders, that phrase carried a specific and serious warning. Grievance 11 of the Declaration of Independence wasn't just about troop deployments. It was about who controls military power, and what happens to liberty when armies operate without the consent of the people they're stationed among. Britain had a rationale: after the Seven Years' War, troops were needed to manage frontier tensions and prevent another costly conflict with Native peoples. But as JJ Nattrass of George Washington University explains, the gap between that rationale and the soldiers' actual conduct  policing colonial cities, intimidating residents  was what colonists experienced on the ground. The Boston Massacre in 1770 gave those fears a name and a date. For Jefferson, the principle was clear: a government that stations armies among its own people without their consent has stopped governing and started occupying. Episode 11 of our 27-part series is live. Join us for Constitution Day Live →  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #StandingArmy

2,000+ attendees. 60 students competing. 20 projects. $5,000 in prizes. Last week, Forest Hills High School in Queens, NY hosted the second annual MyImpact Challenge Civics Fair, and civic participation was on full display. Students presented projects on everything from sustainability education and resume-building workshops to constitutional rights enrichment and deaf community advocacy. Judges from the NYC Mayor's Office, the Queens Borough President's Office, and the NYCDOE Chancellor's Office were in the room. BRI was proud to be there. When students apply constitutional principles and civic virtues to real community problems, this is what happens. See the winners 👇  https://bit.ly/4e6wzbH  #BRI  #MyImpactChallenge  #QueensNY

During the Houston stop of the TFAS Developing Courageous Citizens National Tour, David Bobb of the Bill of Rights Institute, delivered a powerful reminder of what's at stake: We must protect the American experiment by teaching the next generation how to think, not what to think. At TFAS, we take this responsibility seriously. We are dedicated to equipping a pipeline of young leaders who are ready to: 🔹 Wrestle with complex ideas rather than avoid them. 🔹 Debate founding principles using facts, logic and respect. 🔹 Champion liberty and free-market principles in their communities.  #EducationReform  #Leadership  #Networking  #Civics  #TFASOnTour

The Stamp Act didn't just impose a tax. It required an enforcement apparatus to collect it. That apparatus is Grievance 10. As Britain expanded its legislative reach into the colonies through the Stamp Act and the Intolerable Acts, it expanded its administrative presence to match, revenue collectors, appointed officials, and officers appearing in colonial towns to enforce laws the colonists had never consented to. Jefferson described them as "swarms of officers" sent to "eat out their substance." The language is rhetorical, but the argument beneath it is precise: this wasn't just bureaucratic inconvenience. It was a coordinated intrusion into the economic and daily life of the colonies, a direct threat to the livelihoods, freedoms, and dignity of ordinary people. JJ Nattrass of George Washington University explains why Grievance 10 is about more than paperwork in Episode 10 of our 27-part series. Join us for Constitution Day Live:  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #StampAct

Memorial Day is a reminder that the freedoms we often take for granted came at a cost. Pat Tillman left an NFL contract. Michael Murphy gave his life on a mountainside in Afghanistan. John Robert Fox called in artillery on his own position to protect his men. John Basilone returned to combat when he didn't have to. The defenders of Wake Island held on against impossible odds. BRI's free Memorial Day playlist includes lessons and videos on each of these stories, ready to use with students or share with anyone who wants to understand what this day is really about.  https://lnkd.in/e_mxhjXr  #MemorialDay  #CivicsEducation  #AmericanHistory  #FreeResources  #BillofRightsInstitute

"They empower students to wrestle with the meaning of liberty, equality, and justice so that they become principled, productive citizens." That's BRI President David Bobb on what civics and history teachers actually do every day, in every classroom, in every corner of this country. Teacher Appreciation Month is a reminder. BRI tries to make it a practice. This month, there are 2 ways to participate: 📣 Know an exceptional civics or history teacher in grades 6-12? Nominations are open right now for the 2026 National Civics Teacher of the Year award. 📋 Are you a teacher? BRI's annual State of Civics survey is live, and every response directly shapes the resources and programs BRI builds next. If there's a teacher in your life who deserves to be seen, now's the time!  https://bit.ly/42S5uTr  #TeacherAppreciation  #NationalCivicsTeacheroftheYear  #CivicsTeachers  #HistoryTeachers

Who pays your salary determines who you answer to. That's the core of Grievance 9 of the Declaration of Independence. In 1701, Britain had established that judges in Great Britain would be independent of royal control. That protection never extended to the colonies. Then, in 1772, colonists learned that their judges would no longer be paid by local legislatures. They would be paid by the royal treasury, answerable to the king alone. The principle at stake was one Montesquieu had articulated clearly: "There is no liberty if the judiciary power be not separated from the legislative and the executive." Separation of powers is the structural guarantee that the people who decide legal controversies aren't the same people who make and enforce the laws, and aren't beholden to them. By making colonial judges financially dependent on the Crown, King George III had compromised the one institution colonists depended on for impartial justice. Dr. Melissa Dow of the University of West Florida breaks it down in Episode 9 of our 27-part series. Join Constitution Day Live →  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M New episodes every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #SeparationOfPowers

Brown v. Board of Education. 1954. Loving v. Virginia. 1967. Obergefell v. Hodges. 2015. What do these landmark Supreme Court cases have in common? All of them turned on a single amendment, ratified 158 years ago, that Americans are still debating today. The Fourteenth Amendment is arguably the most litigated provision in the entire Constitution. Its Equal Protection Clause and Due Process Clause have shaped American law across more than a century and a half of history, from Reconstruction to the present day. BRI's Fourteenth Amendment playlist gives teachers everything needed to build that understanding from the ground up: primary sources, eLessons, activities, essays, and Homework Help videos. All free, all scholar-reviewed, all classroom-ready. Check it out 👇  https://bit.ly/4nH4vij

English judges had lifetime appointments since 1701. Colonial judges served at the king's pleasure. That gap is Grievance 8 of the Declaration of Independence, and it's more than a procedural complaint. The Act of Settlement (1701) established that judges in England would be appointed for "good behavior," insulating them from political pressure and ensuring they could protect subjects' rights without fear of removal. Colonists wanted the same protection. Several colonies attempted to pass laws establishing identical terms for their own judges. The king consistently blocked them. By 1761, he had made the policy explicit: colonial judges would serve at the pleasure of the Crown. For Jefferson, this wasn't a legal technicality. It was evidence that the king was applying two different standards of English rights depending on where you lived, and that colonists were deliberately receiving less protection under the law than subjects in England. The principle mattered enough that it survived the Revolution entirely. Article III of the U. S. Constitution specifies that appointed federal judges hold their offices during good behavior, the exact protection the king spent decades denying the colonies. Chris Canty of George Washington University breaks it down in Episode 8 of our 27-part series. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live →  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M New episodes every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #SeparationOfPowers
